CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Apr-24-2020 Continued To Jul-01-20 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-16-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT NORTHFIELD INSURANCE COMPANY'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT.

Appearances: Clarke B. Holland of Pacific Law Partners LLP (510.841.7777) for plaintiff and responding party; Ray Tamaddon of Hinshaw & Culbertson LLP (310.909.8078) for defendant and moving party.

Argued and the Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Northfield Insurance Company's demurrer is overruled in its entirety.

Defendant does not show that Plaintiffs are unable to state any cause of action for a breach of contract claim. In a 'mixed' action, in which some of the claims are at least potentially covered and the others are not, the insurer has a duty to defend as to the claims that are at least potentially covered." (Buss v. Sup. Ct. (1997) 16 Cal.4th 35, 47.) It is not clear from the face of the complaint that none of the claims in the underlying action were covered by the insurance policies, and thus the cause of action is not susceptible to demurrer. Further, because Defendant's demurrers to the remaining causes of action are derivative of their claim that there is no viable coverage claim, those demurrers are also overruled.
The court cannot conclude as a matter of law that all of the claims in the underlying complaint are excluded under the habitability exclusion. "Interpretation of an insurance policy is a question of law . . . If the policy language is clear and explicit, it governs . . . Whether language in a contract is ambiguous is a question of law . . . An insurance policy provision is only ambiguous, however, when it is capable of two or more constructions both of which are reasonable." (Baldwin v. AAA N. Cal., Nev. & Utah Ins. Exch. (2016) 1 Cal.App.5th 545, 549-553.) To demonstrate that an exclusion applies, an insurer must provide "conclusive evidence [proving] that the exclusion applies in all possible worlds." (Atlantic Mutual Ins. Co. v. J. Lamb, Inc. (2002) 100 Cal.App.4th 1017, 139.) "Coverage provisions are construed broadly in favor of the insured, while exclusion provisions are construed strictly against the insurer." (National Union Fire Ins. Co. v. Lynette C. (1991) 228 Cal.App.3d 1073, 1077.)

Here, while some of the underlying claims fall squarely within the exclusion of habitability-based claims, others may not. The ninth, tenth, and eleventh causes of action for retaliation, conversion, and trespass to chattels, on their face, are not claims that arise out of a habitability issue. Conway v. Northfield Ins. Co. (N.D. Cal. 2019) 399 F.Supp.3d 950, interpreted the identical policy language involved here, and rejected the argument-indistinguishable from that made by Northfield here-that the so-called catch-all provision of the habitability exclusion should be interpreted as "precluding coverage for any claim in a lawsuit where even one claim concerns habitability." (Id. at 966.) Rather, the court found that "[t]he mere fact that a habitability issue may exist in a complaint is insufficient to satisfy the exclusion." (Id.) Defendant's reliance on S.B.C.C., Inc. v. St. Paul Fire & Marine Ins. Co. (2010) 186 Cal.App.4th 383 is unpersuasive, as that case involved a policy exclusion with different language than the one at issue here.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed form of order. Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR 8600, (E: maria.torreano@gmail.com) (302/EPS)
